AI on the Job

A startup simulation. Not an AI tutorial.
June 10, 2026Date
10:30 – 2:50Time
275 Convent AveGrove School of Engineering
Your Facilitator

Bryan Lozano.
Welcome to Chinatown.

Chief Strategy Officer

Co-founded Decoded Futures at Tech:NYC Foundation. Designed and runs AI training programs for nonprofits and student interns across New York City. Today you are not sitting through a tutorial. You are running a startup simulation.

Today's Lesson
You can't take shortcuts to good.
AI augments problem-solving. It does not replace it. The skill that matters most today is two-fold: Problem Identification and Communication.

Find Your Team

You have been pre-assigned

01 17 teams of 5 or 6. Look for your captain by name.
02 Captains are your most experienced AI users.
03 Captains are co-teaching with me today. They guide. They do not build for you.

01
10:45 – 10:55

First, some definitions

Your captains are co-teaching this one. Pay attention to how they explain it. That is the skill.

Some Definitions

Bryan will call on captains
Translate to plain language. One example each.
How the model works
LLM
Large Language Model. The kind of AI we are using today.
Transformer
The architecture underneath. Reads everything at once instead of word by word.
Pre-training
When the model first learns from massive amounts of internet text.
RLHF
Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback. How humans teach the model what good answers look like.
How you use it
Prompt
What you type to the model.
Context window
Everything the model can hold in its head at once.
Hallucination
When the model is confidently wrong.
Agentic
When AI takes actions, not just answers.
Vibe coding
Building software by describing what you want in plain language, not by writing the code yourself.

Baseline for Safety

10:55 – 11:00
🔒
Protect private info
No PII in prompts
  • No client names, addresses, or financials
  • No social security numbers or IDs
  • Strip identifying details before pasting
  • When in doubt, ask before you paste
👤
Human in the loop
AI proposes. You decide.
  • Review every output before sending
  • AI does not make final decisions about people
  • Use it to draft, not to decide
  • You are accountable for what goes out
🏘
Build for your community
Default outputs erase nuance
  • Generic AI does not speak your community's language
  • Feed it your organization's voice and examples
  • Test outputs with the people you serve
  • If it sounds wrong, it is wrong. Trust that signal.
02
11:00 – 11:20

Identify the Problem

Before any tool, any prompt, any code. The un-shortcut-able skill.

Name Your Pain Point

Fill it in. Write your real one, not a tidy one.
My pain point at work is that (specific issue).

If I had (specific improvement),
I would (see this impact).
Weak
"I don't have enough money / get more money / have lots of impact."
Strong
"I have to answer hundreds of visitor calls and emails all day / answer even half of those automatically / get 5 hours back a week to work on more important museum work."
Three minutes alone. Then share inside your team.

The PITA Task

Four steps
1 Pick a "PITA" task
Not a one-time thing. Something you do every week.
2 Describe it step by step
Apply the "make a sandwich" exercise. Write it out like instructions for someone new.
3 Note how long it takes and how often
Hours per week add up fast.
4 Apply the filters
Next slide.

Finding an AI-Shaped Problem

1
Procedure
Can you write it down before you do it? Procedure = AI candidate. "It depends" = human judgment.
2
Repetition
Does it repeat the same structure every time? Same shape means easier to automate.
3
Risk
What is the cost if it is wrong? Low stakes = automate. High stakes = AI drafts, you decide.
The automation layer

The goal is to find the automation layer within a human-led process.

Never automate the whole task.
Now write your own
Two minutes. Identify one PITA in your personal life and one at your internship. Use the same frame: task, steps, how long, how often.
Personal
Something outside of work
  • A task you do every week at home
  • Write it as steps
  • How long does it take?
  • Does it repeat the same way?
Professional
At your internship
  • A task your supervisor repeats often
  • Something you observed in your first week
  • How long does it probably take?
  • Does it happen on a schedule?
Quick check
Three filters
  • Can you write the steps before doing it?
  • Same structure every time?
  • What happens if it is wrong?
  • If yes, yes, low: you have an AI candidate

What This Looks Like in Practice

Jason Liu
Soft Swerve Ice Cream · 6 locations
Pain point
"I spend Sundays digging through six POS reports trying to find patterns."
AI-shaped
Procedure: yes, he could describe the steps. Repetition: every week, same shape. Risk: low, the data is his own.
What he built
Swerve Intelligence. A custom sales dashboard tracking flavor performance and revenue across all six locations.
He did not build a "shop assistant." He built one thing that answered one question. That is what applying the framework looks like.

Swerve Intelligence

Flavor Performance · Allen Street · This Week

Swerve Intelligence

Sales by Day & Time · All Locations · This Month
03
11:20 – 11:50

The Prompting Framework

The quality of what AI gives you is entirely determined by the quality of what you put in.

Prompting Framework

StepWhat to doExample
01 Role & GoalTell AI who it is and what to accomplish."You are a data analyst. Your goal is to build a sales dashboard from this CSV."
02 StepsBreak the task into clear numbered steps."Step 1: clean the data. Step 2: calculate totals by category. Step 3: build a chart."
03 OutputDescribe exactly what you want back."Give me a single HTML file I can open in my browser with charts and a summary table."
04 ReflectionAsk AI to verify its own work."Check that all category names are consistent and all totals are correct."
When you build with AI, a context file carries this setup once, so you do not retype it every prompt.

Low Prompt vs. High Prompt

Low Prompt
"Help me onboard to my new internship. Tell me what I should know."

No context. The AI does not know your organization, your role, or what you actually need. You get generic advice.
High Prompt
## Role & Goal
You are an onboarding assistant for a new intern. Help me understand my organization, my role, and what success looks like in my first weeks.

## My situation
Organization: [ name ]
My role: [ your title ]
Main responsibilities: [ 2-3 things ]
Tools I will use: [ apps or systems ]

## What I need right now
[ one specific thing, e.g. "write a professional intro email to my team" ]

## Output format
Give me a draft email, a checklist, or a step-by-step plan.

## Self-check
Before responding, confirm the answer is specific to my organization, not generic.
The work you put into the prompt is the work. AI reflects the quality of your instructions, not the other way around.

The Prompt Cookbook

A library of proven, production-ready prompts for nonprofits, organized by use case.
decodedfutures.nyc/prompt-cookbook
Scan to open on your phone
04
11:50 – 12:25

Building with AI

Same framework, applied to building.

What is Vibe Coding

01
You describe what you want in plain language. The model writes the code.
02
You do not need to know syntax. You need to know what good looks like.
03
The work is in the spec, the prompt, and the feedback loop. Not the typing.
04
It is still your job to know if it works. AI does not.

Your Project's Brain

📄
The context markdown file

A plain text markdown file you give the AI before you prompt it. Tells the model who it is, what it is working on, what good looks like, and what the rules are. The model reads it every time so you stop retyping the same setup.

🏗️
What
The project
  • What you are building
  • What it must do
  • Any data or files it uses
🎯
Why
Purpose and audience
  • Who it is for
  • Why it matters to them
  • What good looks like
⚙️
How
Rules and standards
  • Style and conventions
  • Steps or commands to follow
  • What to never do
Same idea in every tool. Different filename: CLAUDE.md · AGENTS.md · GEMINI.md · ChatGPT Projects instructions · Google AI Studio system instructions.

Pick One. Stick With It Today.

Browser-based, no install
🌐Google AI Studio
💬ChatGPT Projects
⚙️Replit (browser)
✨Lovable
Terminal or editor
◆Claude Code
💻Codex CLI
✎️Cursor
◆Gemini CLI
No matter which one you pick, you still have to learn how to prompt it effectively. That is the next 20 minutes.

Quick Setup Check

Five minutes
If something fails, switch to browser.
Node installed?Terminal: node --version (need 18+, Codex needs 22+)
Git installed?Terminal: git --version
Tool installed?See your captain.
Context file ready?Open the handout. Use the template.
Set a 5-minute timer. Captains float. Anyone still stuck moves to AI Studio.

Now Build 002

Same idea. Different problem.
Edit World Clock +
Today, same time
New York

Tokyo

Same idea. Different problem.
  • Five cities only
  • The labels are the build: "Today, same time," "Tomorrow, +5HRS," "Today, -3HRS"
  • All relative to your local time
What did you have to add to your context.md to make this work? That is the lesson.

Build Hour: The Rules

1
Spec first, code second. Write the Role & Goal / Steps / Output / Reflection before anyone opens a tool.
2
One sentence problem. If your sentence has "and" in it, you are doing too much.
3
Keyboard rule. Navigator never touches it. Builder never builds silently.
4
Demo includes what broke. Not just what worked.
5
What shortcut did you try? Be ready to name it.

Pick Your Roles Before You Build

B
Builder
  • Drives the keyboard
  • Must be non-technical
  • Asks questions out loud
  • Does not silently code
N
Navigator
  • Cannot touch the keyboard
  • Captain takes this seat by default
  • Communication is the whole job
  • Directs, does not type
P
PM
  • Tracks decisions
  • Watches the clock
  • Prepares the demo
  • Writes down what worked and what broke
12:25
GROUP
PHOTO
Smile. You are going to want to see this in a year.
12:30 – 1:15
LUNCH
Outside
Be back at 1:15 sharp. Build hour starts on time.

Pick a real problem. Not a hypothetical one.

One person on your team names a real PITA. From school. From a side hustle. From your group chat. From your job last summer. From the internship search you just finished.

Not "it would be cool if." A real thing that wastes your time every week.

Run it through the framework
1
MADLIB the pain point. Be specific.
2
PITA: what is the repetitive task underneath?
3
Three filters: is it AI-shaped? Procedure, repetition, risk.
4
One sentence problem. If your sentence has "and" in it, you are doing too much.
5
Write your context.md. Then prompt.
The shortcut is asking AI what to build. The work is knowing your own problem.
Build Hour
60:00
Click anywhere or press Space to start  ·  R to reset
Spec first.
Keyboard rule.
Demo what broke.

Demos · 30 min

What we want to see

  • One thing that worked
  • One thing that broke. Exactly where.
  • One thing your team learned about communicating with each other
Cluster demos first (3 min each within your cluster). One team per cluster presents to the full room.

AI on the Job: Tips for Building

01
Make the problem smaller. What is the MVP?
02
Spend more time on the problem, the prompt, and the output than you think you should, before pulling in vibe-coding tools.
03
Build for yourself before building for others.
04
Start small. Complexity scales with audience size and interface complexity.
05
Ask yourself: "Am I still learning?"
06
Can you replicate the process on your own? Can you teach someone else what you did, and why?

AI on the Job: How to Talk to Your Manager

The mindset
Junior interns worry too much about the codebase. A startup runs on curiosity, learning the process, and the people. Lead with those.
Be curious, out loud
Ask why, not just how. "What problem are we really solving here? Why did we choose this approach?"
Learn the process
Understand how work actually ships. "Can you walk me through how this goes from idea to done? Where could I help?"
Invest in the people
Relationships matter more than the code. "Who should I be learning from here? What would make your week easier?"
When you spot an AI-shaped task, show a small proof of concept built without private data. And ask about the AI policy before you use a tool.
What shortcut did you try today?
And what did it cost you?
Your commitment: What workflow will you map at your internship this week?
Thank you. Now go build something for you first.
Before you go
